reforms.description: The government has enhanced tax preferences for R&D investment, allowing a tax credit of 20% on R&D expenses, which can increase to 40% for incremental expenses. Additionally, corporate income tax breaks for investment in R&D will be further enhanced, aiming to increase Spain's R&D investment to align more closely with the EU average.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Enhancing tax incentives for R&D investment is expected to stimulate private sector R&D spending, thereby fostering innovation and productivity.
reforms.status_evidence: The tax preferences for R&D investment have been put into effect as of 2000.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform significantly alters the tax landscape for R&D investment, encouraging increased private sector engagement in R&D activities.
reforms.source_quote: Corporate income tax breaks for investment in R&D will be further enhanced, with the aim of bringing Spain’s comparatively low share of R&D investment closer to the EU average.

reforms.description: Public investment spending in R&D is set to increase by 10.5%, reflecting a commitment to enhance the country's research capabilities. Additionally, Spain has shifted its public support to industry from sector-specific aid to horizontal policy programmes, significantly increasing the share of support directed towards R&D activities, which rose by more than 50% between 1993 and 1997.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increasing public investment in R&D is expected to enhance research capabilities and stimulate innovation across various sectors.
reforms.status_evidence: The increase in public investment and the shift in support strategy have been executed as of 1997.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform represents a significant shift in public funding strategy, aiming to bolster R&D investment substantially.
reforms.source_quote: The launch of the European Single Market called for a change in the pattern of public support to industry, to enforce cross-country discipline and prevent distortions in competition.

reforms.description: The Spanish authorities introduced a series of supportive financial regulations to facilitate business access to capital markets, particularly for small and young companies, including easing listing requirements and liberalising venture capital regulations. Additionally, a draft law on venture capital companies was approved to enhance flexibility in the management of venture capital portfolios for small and medium-sized enterprises.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Facilitating access to capital for startups is expected to promote innovation and entrepreneurship, leading to economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The supportive financial regulations and draft law have been enacted as of 1998.
reforms.importance_rationale: The introduction of supportive financial regulations and a draft law represents a significant overhaul of the venture capital landscape in Spain.
reforms.source_quote: To help private businesses – especially small and young, more risky, companies – to take advantage of the opportunities of expanding securities markets, the Spanish authorities have introduced a series of supportive financial regulations.

reforms.description: A new Venture Capital Law has been introduced to increase tax incentives for venture capital funds, enhancing their attractiveness and regulatory oversight.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increasing tax incentives for venture capital funds is expected to attract more investment into startups, fostering innovation and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The Venture Capital Law has been enacted and is in effect as of 2000.
reforms.importance_rationale: The introduction of the Venture Capital Law represents a significant reform in the regulatory framework for venture capital in Spain.
reforms.source_quote: New Venture Capital Law that increases tax incentives to venture capital funds and puts them under the supervision of the stock market regulator.
